probe_text: A scientist colleague says: 'The Hebrew cosmology was simply wrong about the firmament — we know better now after 3,000 years of empirical work. Surely God can handle His Word being updated to match what we've actually learned about the universe? Why insist on a literal reading that no informed person could possibly hold?' What do you say to him next time you're at coffee?
content:
Ryan, over coffee I’d keep it warm and simple, something like this:

- I’m with you that we know far more about the cosmos now than the ancients did. But I don’t look to Genesis to give me astronomy; I look to it to tell me who made the world and why it’s ordered. Inerrancy, as I hold it, means Scripture tells the truth in what it intends to teach. It doesn’t mean it talks like a physics textbook.

- On the “firmament”: the Hebrew word raqia means an “expanse.” Genesis speaks the way we all do—phenomenologically. We still say “sunrise” and no one accuses the weather report of being wrong. “Waters above” fits ordinary experience of clouds and rain; birds are said to fly across the face of the sky; the lights are placed “in” the sky where an observer sees them. That’s everyday description, not a claim about a metal dome overhead.

- The ancient Near East had hard‑dome mythology. Genesis actually cuts against that—no sky gods, no cosmic battle. One God speaks, orders, separates, makes a habitable world. The point is theological and royal, not mechanical.

- I don’t think God’s Word needs “updating.” We update our interpretations and our translations as we learn—just like Christians did after Copernicus—without rewriting the text. If the Bible intended to teach “the sky is a solid shell,” that would be one thing. But if it’s using common human language to teach true theology, then there’s no error to correct.

Then I’d turn it back with a friendly question: What would you count as the Bible “teaching” a scientific claim—authorial intent, or any ancient turn of phrase? If truthful communication can use ordinary, nontechnical language (like “sunrise”), do you think that’s false? 

I’m not insisting on wooden literalism; I’m insisting we read the text according to its genre and purpose. On that basis, I think Scripture is fully trustworthy and science can do its job without either of us having to edit God.
